items.prompt_md: You're maintaining a window that must always hold exactly `k` elements. When the window slides forward by one position, what do you do?
options: Add the new element entering the window and subtract the element leaving it
Recompute the sum of all k elements from scratch
Only add the new element — nothing ever leaves the window
Remove the smallest element currently in the window
items.answer: Add the new element entering the window and subtract the element leaving it
items.explanation_md: A fixed-size window keeps a running total. Each slide is one addition (the new right-edge element) and one subtraction (the old left-edge element) — that's the whole update.

items.prompt_md: Why is recomputing the sum of every k-length window from scratch considered wasteful?
options: It turns an O(n) scan into O(n·k) work, since each of the ~n windows costs O(k) to sum
It uses O(n) extra memory that a running sum wouldn't need
It only produces correct answers when k is even
It requires sorting the array before scanning
items.answer: It turns an O(n) scan into O(n·k) work, since each of the ~n windows costs O(k) to sum
items.explanation_md: There are roughly n windows, and summing k elements each time costs O(k), so recomputing from scratch is O(n·k). Sliding the sum forward with one add and one subtract collapses that to O(n).

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of finding the maximum sum among all k-length subarrays of an n-element array using a sliding window that updates the sum incrementally?
options: O(n)
O(n·k)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: Every element is added to the window exactly once and removed exactly once as the window slides across the array, so the total work is proportional to n, not n·k.

items.prompt_md: What is the typical space complexity of a fixed-size sliding window that only tracks a running sum (or count) of the current window?
options: O(k)
O(n)
O(n·k)
items.explanation_md: You only need a few scalar variables — the running sum, the current bounds, and the best value seen — regardless of how large k or n is. If you instead need per-element state (like character counts), space grows to O(k).

items.prompt_md: A fixed-size window spans indices `[left, right]` and must always contain exactly k elements once it's full. How does `left` behave as `right` advances?
options: It advances in lockstep with right, one step for one step, so the window size never exceeds k
It only moves when the running sum turns negative
It stays pinned at index 0 for the entire scan
It moves backward whenever a duplicate value is found
items.answer: It advances in lockstep with right, one step for one step, so the window size never exceeds k
items.explanation_md: Unlike a variable window (which shrinks only when a condition is violated), a fixed window has one unconditional rule: once full, every step advances both edges together so the size stays exactly k.

items.prompt_md: A fixed-window implementation uses `if right - left < k` to decide whether the window has "filled up" yet, when it should use `if right - left + 1 < k`. What's the effect of this off-by-one mistake?
options: The first window it evaluates is one element short (or long), producing a wrong sum or average
It causes an infinite loop that never terminates
It has no effect, since the window size is enforced elsewhere
It only produces wrong answers when k is an even number
items.answer: The first window it evaluates is one element short (or long), producing a wrong sum or average
items.explanation_md: Window bounds are inclusive, so the count of elements between left and right is `right - left + 1`, not `right - left`. Getting this off by one shifts when the window is considered "full" and corrupts the first result.

items.prompt_md: You maintain a running `window_sum` for a fixed k-size window. Besides adding the newly entering element, what else must happen on every slide once the window is full?
options: Subtract the element that just left the window — the one that was at the old left edge
Divide window_sum by k to keep it normalized
Reset window_sum to zero before adding the new element
Subtract the new element instead of adding it
items.answer: Subtract the element that just left the window — the one that was at the old left edge
items.explanation_md: The whole point of sliding rather than recomputing is that only one element enters and one leaves per step. Forgetting the subtraction silently turns your fixed window into a growing one.

items.prompt_md: Why does the fixed-size sliding window avoid the O(n·k) cost of the brute-force approach?
options: Each element is added to the window exactly once and removed exactly once across the whole scan, so total work is O(n)
It skips over some elements of the array entirely
It sorts the array first in O(n log n), which dominates the cost
It caches every window's sum in a hash map to avoid recomputation
items.answer: Each element is added to the window exactly once and removed exactly once across the whole scan, so total work is O(n)
items.explanation_md: Brute force redoes O(k) work per window for ~n windows. The sliding version does O(1) work per step (one add, one subtract) for n steps — O(n) total.

items.prompt_md: In your fixed-window code, you add the newly entering element to `window_sum` on every slide, but you forget to subtract the element that just left. What happens to `window_sum` as the scan progresses?
options: It keeps growing incorrectly, effectively becoming the sum of an ever-expanding window instead of a fixed k-length one
It becomes negative almost immediately
The program crashes with an out-of-bounds index error
Nothing changes — window_sum stays correct because addition and subtraction cancel out
items.answer: It keeps growing incorrectly, effectively becoming the sum of an ever-expanding window instead of a fixed k-length one
items.explanation_md: Without the subtraction, every element you've ever visited stays counted in the sum. The bug doesn't crash anything — it just silently produces a running total over a growing window instead of a fixed one, which is why it's easy to miss.

items.prompt_md: You need to count how many windows of size k in an n-element array have a sum at or above some threshold, updating the sum incrementally as the window slides. What's the overall time complexity?
options: O(n), since each window's sum is derived from the previous one in O(1) amortized work
O(n·k), because each window must be recomputed
O(n^2) in the worst case
O(2^n), since every subset of size k must be checked
items.answer: O(n), since each window's sum is derived from the previous one in O(1) amortized work
items.explanation_md: Deriving each new window's sum from the previous one costs O(1) per step. Across n steps that's O(n) total, regardless of what k is.

items.prompt_md: While sliding, how many elements must a fixed-size window of size k contain once it has filled up for the first time?
options: Exactly k elements — never more, never fewer
At least k elements, but it's allowed to grow further
At most k elements, but it may shrink below k
Exactly n minus k elements
items.answer: Exactly k elements — never more, never fewer
items.explanation_md: That's what makes it a fixed window: once full, the size is invariant. A variable window (next lesson's topic) is the one that's allowed to grow and shrink based on a condition.

items.prompt_md: If you compute the average of every k-length window in an n-element array by summing all k elements fresh for each window, what's the time complexity?
options: O(n·k)
O(n)
O(n + k)
O(log n)
items.answer: O(n·k)
items.explanation_md: Roughly n windows exist, and each naive sum costs O(k), giving O(n·k) total — the exact cost the incremental slide-and-update technique is designed to eliminate.

items.prompt_md: Given an array of daily temperatures, find the maximum total temperature across any 7 consecutive days.
options: Recompute each window from scratch
Slide a fixed-size window, add/remove one element per step
Hash map of complements
Two pointers converging from the ends
items.answer: Slide a fixed-size window, add/remove one element per step
items.explanation_md: "Any 7 consecutive days" is a contiguous subarray of a *given, fixed length* — the fingerprint for a fixed-size sliding window. Slide the window and update the sum incrementally instead of resumming each block of 7.

items.prompt_md: Given a string and an integer k, find the substring of length exactly k that contains the fewest repeated characters.
options: Two pointers converging from the ends
Recompute each window from scratch
Slide a fixed-size window, add/remove one element per step
Hash map of complements
items.answer: Slide a fixed-size window, add/remove one element per step
items.explanation_md: The substring length is pinned at exactly k, so slide a window of that fixed length across the string, updating a character-count map by adding the entering char and removing the leaving one.

items.prompt_md: Given a sorted array of integers, find two numbers that add up to a given target value.
options: Slide a fixed-size window, add/remove one element per step
Hash map of complements
Two pointers converging from the ends
Recompute each window from scratch
items.answer: Two pointers converging from the ends
items.explanation_md: Trap: there's no window of a given length here, and the array being sorted means you can start one pointer at each end and converge toward the target — that's two pointers, not a fixed window.

items.prompt_md: Given an unsorted array of integers, find two numbers that add up to a given target value.
options: Slide a fixed-size window, add/remove one element per step
Recompute each window from scratch
Two pointers converging from the ends
Hash map of complements
items.answer: Hash map of complements
items.explanation_md: Trap: without a sort, two pointers don't work, and there's no fixed-length contiguous window in play. Track seen values in a hash map and look up each element's complement (target minus the current value).

items.prompt_md: Given an array of integers and an integer k, find the maximum average value of any subarray of length k.
options: Recompute each window from scratch
Two pointers converging from the ends
Hash map of complements
Slide a fixed-size window, add/remove one element per step
items.answer: Slide a fixed-size window, add/remove one element per step
items.explanation_md: "Any subarray of length k" is the textbook fixed-window trigger. Maintain a running sum, divide by k for the average, and update it by one add/one subtract per slide rather than resumming each window.

items.prompt_md: Given a sorted array with duplicate values, remove the duplicates in place so each value appears once, and return the new length.
options: Hash map of complements
Slide a fixed-size window, add/remove one element per step
Two pointers converging from the ends
Recompute each window from scratch
items.answer: Two pointers converging from the ends
items.explanation_md: Trap: there's no fixed-length window here — a slow pointer marks the next unique-value slot while a fast pointer scans ahead, both moving forward through the sorted array. That's two pointers, not a sliding window.

items.prompt_md: Given an array of n integers and an integer k, count how many contiguous subarrays of length exactly k have a sum greater than or equal to a given threshold.
options: Two pointers converging from the ends
Slide a fixed-size window, add/remove one element per step
Recompute each window from scratch
Hash map of complements
items.answer: Slide a fixed-size window, add/remove one element per step
items.explanation_md: "Length exactly k" is the fixed-window signal. Slide the window across the array, update the running sum incrementally, and check the threshold once per position.

items.prompt_md: Given an unsorted array of integers, determine whether it contains any duplicate value.
options: Slide a fixed-size window, add/remove one element per step
Hash map of complements
Recompute each window from scratch
Two pointers converging from the ends
items.answer: Hash map of complements
items.explanation_md: Trap: no fixed length is given and the array isn't sorted, so track values seen so far in a hash set as you scan — the general "hash map / hash set over an unsorted array" family, not a windowed or two-pointer scan.

items.prompt_md: Given a string s and an integer k, find the length-k substring containing the highest number of vowels.
options: Hash map of complements
Two pointers converging from the ends
Recompute each window from scratch
Slide a fixed-size window, add/remove one element per step
items.answer: Slide a fixed-size window, add/remove one element per step
items.explanation_md: The substring length k is fixed, so slide a window of that length across s, adding a vowel count when a vowel enters and subtracting when one leaves, instead of rescanning each substring.

items.prompt_md: Given two separate sorted arrays, determine whether there exists a pair — one element from each array — that sums to a given target.
options: Recompute each window from scratch
Slide a fixed-size window, add/remove one element per step
Two pointers converging from the ends
Hash map of complements
items.answer: Two pointers converging from the ends
items.explanation_md: Trap: no contiguous window of fixed length is involved. Both arrays are sorted, so walk one pointer up from the start of one array and one pointer down from the end of the other, converging toward the target.

items.prompt_md: Given an array of stock prices and a fixed holding period of k days, find the buy day that maximizes profit if the stock is sold exactly k days later.
options: Two pointers converging from the ends
Recompute each window from scratch
Hash map of complements
Slide a fixed-size window, add/remove one element per step
items.answer: Slide a fixed-size window, add/remove one element per step
items.explanation_md: "Exactly k days later" pins the buy-sell gap to a fixed length, so this reduces to comparing `price[i + k] - price[i]` as a window of span k slides across the array, rather than checking every possible pair.

items.prompt_md: Given an unsorted list of exam scores, determine whether any two scores sum to exactly 100.
options: Slide a fixed-size window, add/remove one element per step
Recompute each window from scratch
Hash map of complements
Two pointers converging from the ends
items.answer: Hash map of complements
items.explanation_md: Trap: the list isn't sorted and there's no fixed contiguous length in play, so two pointers and a sliding window both fail here. Record seen scores in a hash map and check for `100 - score` as you scan.
